bills.title: Offenses Against Public Order; the punishment for the offense of obstructing highways, streets, sidewalks, or other public passages; increase
bills.summary: The bill defines the legal consequences for individuals who, without legal authority, purposely or recklessly obstruct a public passage so that it becomes impassable without 'unreasonable inconvenience or hazard' and refuse to move after an official request. A significant addition in this bill is the introduction of civil consequences. Beyond criminal fines, penalties, or restitution, a person found guilty of this offense can be held civilly liable for damages resulting from the obstruction.

bills.title: DNA Sampling; the collection of DNA samples from any individual in a detention facility charged with a misdemeanor or felony who is subject to an immigration detainer notice; require
bills.summary: The bill requires DNA samples to be collected via oral swab or other noninvasive procedures from individuals charged with a misdemeanor or felony who are also subject to an immigration detainer notice. This requirement applies if the individual is not taken into custody by U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) within 48 hours of detention, excluding weekends and holidays. Samples must be collected by the detention facility (such as county jails, municipal facilities, or state prisons) detaining the individual or the entity supervising them.

bills.title: Education; enact policies and procedures for use of personal electronic devices at school and school sponsored events by students in grades nine through twelve; require local school systems
bills.summary: Mandates that Georgia public schools and local school systems implement policies to restrict student access to personal electronic devices during the school day to foster a 'distraction-free education'. K-8 Students: Access must be prohibited during the school day no later than July 1, 2026. High School (Grades 9-12): Access must be prohibited no later than July 1, 2027. Local systems must adopt and revise their specific policies and procedures by January 1 of each year, beginning in 2026.

bills.title: Georgia Early Literacy Act of 2026; enact
bills.summary: Comprehensive legislative effort to advance statewide literacy through evidence-based instruction aligned with the science of reading. The state will provide grants to fund one literacy coach per eligible school (K-3) to provide onsite, nonevaluative professional learning and side-by-side classroom support. All early literacy instruction must be focused on structured literacy and foundational skills, anchored by high-quality materials aligned with the science of reading. Local systems must annually certify that their instructional materials are high-quality and aligned with state standards.

bills.title: General appropriations; State Fiscal Year July 1, 2025 - June 30, 2026
bills.summary: FY 2026 state appropriations budget ($37.7 billion). Funds K–12 education, corrections, healthcare, and Hurricane Helene recovery. Includes $141 million to launch the Georgia Promise Scholarship (school voucher) program.

bills.title: 'The Georgia Promise Scholarship Act'; establishment of promise scholarship accounts
bills.summary: Georgia Promise Scholarship Act. Allows families of students in the lowest-performing 25% of public schools to receive up to $6,500 in state funds for private school tuition or homeschooling. Passed the House 91–82, largely along party lines. Program launched Fall 2025.

bills.title: Income tax; reduce rate of tax
bills.summary: Reduces Georgia's flat personal income tax rate from 5.39% to 5.19%, retroactive to January 1, 2025. Sets automatic annual 0.10% cuts until the rate reaches 4.99% — continuing Georgia's multi-year shift to a flat tax structure.

bills.title: Income tax; one-time tax credit for taxpayers who filed returns for both 2023 and 2024 taxable years; provide
bills.summary: Authorizes a one-time income tax rebate of approximately $1 billion — up to $500 for married couples filing jointly, $375 for head of household, and $250 for single filers. Georgia's third consecutive year returning surplus funds to taxpayers.

bills.title: Elections; text portions of ballots shall be counted for vote tabulation and recounts purposes; provide
bills.summary: Amends Georgia election law: defines grounds for challenging voter eligibility (death, out-of-state registration, non-residential address), requires counties to report absentee ballot results within one hour of polls closing, and restricts non-citizens from employment in county election offices.
